trajectory_summary: The call opened with a warm, self-disclosing customer who had real solar interest, a prior competitive touchpoint, and an engineering curiosity that could have been channeled toward a design appointment. Energy leaked steadily as the rep responded to each of Harry's tangents with flat acknowledgment or literal correction, never anchoring bill pain, never creating curiosity, and never attempting the changed_mind rebuttal when Harry closed the door at 03:15. The call ended with a clean opt-out that the rep accepted without a single recovery attempt.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but this call should not be tagged as a qualified lead or a bookable outcome. No bill, homeowner status, credit score, roof age, or title data was collected. Harry is a lead with real potential that was abandoned before qualification could be completed — not a qualified lead hiding behind a misconception.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Changed-mind rebuttal — zero recovery attempts before release
primary_coaching_focus.quote: alright. Well, anyway, the answer is no. I'm not interested.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland accepted a soft refusal from a warm, self-disclosing customer who had never seen an Ion design, without attempting the one rebuttal the schema defines for exactly this moment. Harry's 'anyway' signals a tangent close, not a considered decision. This is the call's highest-leverage coaching gap because it is the last moment where the outcome was still recoverable — and Holland didn't try.

key_moments.quote: yeah. The the government, they don't pay for solar, unfortunately.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the highest-leverage flip opportunity in the call. Harry's 'freebie' frame is not ignorance — it's a cost-pain signal in disguise. He wants solar to be free because he doesn't want to pay his current bill either. The rep's 'unfortunately they don't pay for solar' closes the door on the frame instead of walking through it. The inversion was available: his current bill IS the cost he's already paying, every month, with no asset at the end. Reactive read holds — there is no evidence the rep was setting up a later move; the call never returned to this frame.

key_moments.quote: I'm I'm I'm a mechanical engineer, and I'm thinking, hey. I have solar lights all around my patio, and they work really good. Why can't I put a portable solar field in my backyard, which is huge, and feed it into my house?
key_moments.stewart_read: Harry volunteers two high-value signals in one breath: (1) he has a 'huge' backyard — a ground-mount feasibility signal that a closer could use to design a larger system than a roof-only install would support; (2) he is an engineer who has already thought through DIY solar, which means his interest is genuine and his engagement with a real design would be high. The rep responds only to the business-model question ('we don't sell equipment like that') and misses both signals entirely.

key_moments.quote: So we we actually we don't sell, like, the equipment. Like, we don't just sell our equipment like that. We only sell the solar system, and then we we will come out and and install it and then everything.
key_moments.stewart_read: Three utterances explaining what Ion does and doesn't sell. Per setter_scope_discipline.scope_creep_signals, 'rep debating company operations' is a named anti-pattern. The rep is not selling solar here — she's defending Ion's business model to a customer who asked a genuine question. The universal escape hatch ('that's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') was available and unused. Reactive read holds — this is mild scope creep, not a strategic setup.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I know. The the calls definitely get old. I agree.
key_moments.stewart_read: Harry has just described a world of AI-generated scam content impersonating celebrities to sell supplements — a general societal anxiety, not a personal scam disclosure. The rep commiserates ('I agree') rather than using the moment to distinguish Ion from the noise. The scam-anxiety signal is present even without a personal encounter: Harry is telling the rep that he is primed to distrust unsolicited contact. The correct move is to reframe the appointment as customer-initiated — Harry filled out a form, Ion is responding to his request, not cold-calling him. Commiseration validates his distrust without resolving it.
